text: On a golden morning in the United States, a creative team set out to build a floating school on an old oil rig, a project that seemed endless at first. The preliminary plan, drawn from a worn textbook, set each parameter with precise care. We would not construe the river as a barrier but as a friendly road; a ferryboat would carry every passenger across the distance, and a graceful colonnade of bamboo would shade the entry. Our savings were modest, so we had to conserve every bit, and a formal promissory note would empower the work. Regularly, a referee-like inspector would visit for a checkup, give his verdict, and instruct us on rectification wherever a flaw might jeopardise safety. We did not presume the task easy. A pessimistic critic, an outcast from the local council, would commonly call the idea worthless and malicious, but we would not let his criticism shatter our resolve. With fidelity to the children, we would strive to win, conscious that a single careless switch could trap a curious child. To justify the cost, we kept a variable budget and a precise discussion log, and we would not yield to vice or wicked shortcuts. A grateful adoptive family, who had once felt lonely and treated like a nomad, donated a brown medal of honor to hang above the door. We would migrate desks by ladder, bridle our impatience, and meditate in quiet leisure when the strain grew chronic. A baby-sitter taught a tutorial on first aid; a professional from the post-office helped us mail forms; and new legislation, after a close election, eased the way. The agricultural village nearby gave us each a pea-green lunch box, and the children, attentively, would echo every lesson. We had to invent a new desk that would fold flat, and we would wrestle with each design until it worked. The questions of the young could elicit fresh ideas from us all. We gave the school a simple title, painted in white. We added zinc panels to the roof to relieve the heat. One could presume that an elephant of doubt would block us, but we pressed on. A small starfish, a habit of meditation, the prevalent hope of the people: all helped us reach the summit. The intensity of our effort, in fair proportion to the dream, would not let us fly from duty. No invasion of doubt, no snore of laziness, could stop us. Marxism, the brave above-mentioned ideals, the further reach of justice, all met in one wish: to win for the children. The wind would echo, the river would hit the hull, and still we built, brave and free.
translation: 在美国一个金色的早晨，一支富有创意的团队着手在一座旧石油钻井平台上建造一所漂浮的学校，这个项目起初似乎没有尽头。初步方案取自一本破旧的教科书，以精确的细心设定了每一个参数。我们不会把河流理解为一道屏障，而是一条友善的道路；一艘渡轮会载着每位乘客跨越那段距离，一排优雅的竹制柱廊会为入口遮荫。我们的积蓄不多，所以必须节省每一点，一张正式的本票将为这项工作提供资金保障。一位裁判般的检查员会定期前来体检，做出他的裁定，并在任何可能危及安全的缺陷处指导我们整改。我们并不臆断这件事容易。一位悲观的批评者，本地议会的一个弃儿，常常把这个想法说成毫无价值且居心不良，但我们不会让他的批评粉碎我们的决心。出于对孩子们的忠诚，我们会奋力争取胜利，意识到一个粗心的开关就可能困住一个好奇的孩子。为了证明开支合理，我们保留了一份可变动的预算和一份精确的讨论记录，我们绝不会屈从于恶习或邪恶的捷径。一户心怀感激的收养家庭，曾经感到孤独、像游牧者一样被对待，捐出一枚棕色的荣誉勋章挂在门上。我们会用梯子搬运课桌，克制我们的急躁，并在劳累变得长期化时于宁静的闲暇中冥想。一位保姆讲授了一堂急救辅导课；邮局的一位专业人员帮我们邮寄表格；在一场势均力敌的选举之后，新的立法也为我们铺平了道路。附近的农业村庄给我们每人一个豆绿色的饭盒，孩子们会专注地回响每一节课。我们得发明一种能折平的新课桌，并会反复琢磨每一个设计，直到它行得通。孩子们的提问能从我们所有人身上引出新鲜的想法。我们给这所学校起了一个简单的名字，用白漆写上。我们在屋顶加上锌板以缓解暑热。人们或许会臆测，一头疑虑的大象会挡住我们，但我们继续前进。一只小海星、一种冥想的习惯、人们普遍的希望：这一切都帮助我们抵达顶峰。我们努力的强度，与梦想相称地成比例，不会让我们逃离职责。没有任何疑虑的入侵，没有任何懒惰的鼾声，能够阻止我们。马克思主义、上述那些勇敢的理想、正义更深远的伸展，全都汇聚于一个愿望：为孩子们赢得胜利。风会回响，河水会拍打船体，而我们依然建造着，勇敢而自由。
